response.task: Create an ELAR/PARADISEC-compatible archive for a Papuan language with <200 speakers, integrating field recordings, interlinear glossed texts (IGTs), paradigm elicitation data from 6 workers using 4 transcription conventions, and producing FLEx-compatible lexical database, typological grammatical sketch, curated time‑aligned text collection, cross‑references, consistency checks, and metadata on consent, recording conditions, and dialectal variation.

solution_steps.title: Collect raw materials
solution_steps.description: Gather all audio files, IGTs, paradigm sheets, and transcription notes from the six fieldworkers. Store them in a single repository with provenance metadata (worker ID, date, location).

solution_steps.title: Standardize file naming and directory structure
solution_steps.description: Create a consistent folder hierarchy: /audio/, /texts/, /lexicon/, /metadata/. Assign each item a unique identifier following ELAR/PARADISEC guidelines.

solution_steps.title: Normalize transcription conventions
solution_steps.description: Convert IPA narrow, IPA broad, practical orthography, and ad‑hoc romanization into a unified Unicode representation (UTF‑8). Use mapping scripts to preserve phonetic detail while ensuring consistency.

solution_steps.title: Generate FLEx-compatible lexical database
solution_steps.description: Create an XML file with entries for all lemmas, including fields: lemma, part of speech, glosses, phonological form (normalized), and cross‑references to text IDs. Validate against FLEx schema.

solution_steps.title: Build typological grammatical sketch
solution_steps.description: Compile a structured document (JSON or LaTeX) organized by morphophonology, argument structure, clause combining, and information structure. Cite specific IGTs and paradigm items for each observation.

solution_steps.title: Time‑align audio with texts
solution_steps.description: Use Praat or ELAN scripts to link each IGT line to its corresponding time segment in the audio, producing synchronized XML/ELAN files for the archive.

solution_steps.title: Cross‑reference lexical entries and text attestations
solution_steps.description: Run an automated matching script that flags occurrences of lemmas from the FLEx database within IGTs. Generate a report listing all citations and highlight missing links for manual review.

solution_steps.title: Validate morpheme segmentation consistency
solution_steps.description: Compare segment boundaries across transcribers using a comparison tool; log discrepancies for manual reconciliation to ensure uniform morphological analysis.

solution_steps.title: Compile metadata layer
solution_steps.description: Create an XML/JSON file recording speaker consent status, recording conditions (location, equipment, time), and dialectal variation notes. Link each entry to the corresponding audio/text via unique IDs.

solution_steps.title: Package archive for ELAR/PARADISEC submission
solution_steps.description: Zip all files with a manifest, README, and metadata schema. Ensure compliance with ELAR/PARADISEC format requirements before upload.

quality_self_assessment.justification: All steps are evidence‑based, align with ELAR/PARADISEC standards, and cover required outputs (lexicon, grammar sketch, time‑aligned texts, metadata).

output.internal_reasoning: I mapped the task requirements to a stepwise workflow that follows ELAR/PARADISEC guidelines, ensures cross‑referencing and consistency checks, and includes metadata capture.
output.formatted_prompt:
Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'computational_linguistics', 'task': 'Gather linguistic field recordings, interlinear glossed texts, and paradigm elicitation data from a Papuan language with no prior written documentation and fewer than 200 speakers, sourced from 6 fieldworkers using 4 different transcription conventions (IPA narrow, IPA broad, practical orthography, ad-hoc romanization). Structure the materials into an ELAR/PARADISEC-compatible archive with FLEx-interoperable lexical database, grammatical sketch organized by typological feature (morphophonology, argument structure, clause combining, and information structure), and curated text collection with time-aligned audio. Polish with cross-references between lexical entries and text attestations, consistency validation of morpheme segmentation across transcribers, and a metadata layer capturing speaker consent status, recording conditions, and dialectal variation notes.'}

response.task: Create an ELAR/PARADISEC-compatible archive for a unwritten Papuan language with <200 speakers, integrating field recordings, interlinear glosses, paradigm data from 6 workers using 4 transcription conventions, and producing FLEx-compatible lexical database, typological grammatical sketch, curated time-aligned texts, cross-references, consistency checks, and metadata on consent, recording conditions, and dialectal variation.

steps.result: All audio files, interlinear glossed texts (IGTs), paradigm elicitation sheets, and transcription notes from the 6 fieldworkers are gathered in a single repository with clear provenance metadata.

steps.action: Standardize file naming and directory structure
steps.result: A consistent folder hierarchy (e.g., /audio/, /texts/, /lexicon/, /metadata/) is established, following ELAR/PARADISEC guidelines, ensuring each item can be uniquely identified.

steps.action: Convert all transcriptions to a unified format
steps.result: Using a script that maps IPA narrow/broad, practical orthography, and ad-hoc romanization into a common Unicode representation (e.g., UTF-8 with diacritics), each line of IGTs is aligned with its audio segment.
A .xml file containing entries for all lemmas, with fields for lemma, part-of-speech, glosses, phonological forms (in the unified transcription), and cross-references to text IDs is generated.

A structured document (e.g., JSON or LaTeX) that organizes observations by morphophonology, argument structure, clause combining, and information structure, each section citing specific IGTs and paradigm items.
Using Praat or ELAN scripts, each IGT line is linked to its corresponding time segment in the audio, producing a synchronized XML/ELAN file for the archive.

steps.action: Cross-reference lexical entries and text attestations
steps.result: Automated matching of lemmas in the FLEx database with occurrences in the IGTs, generating a report that lists all citations and flags missing links.
A comparison tool checks segment boundaries across transcribers; discrepancies are logged for manual review, ensuring uniform morphological analysis.
An XML/JSON file records speaker consent status, recording conditions (location, equipment, time), and dialectal variation notes, linked to each audio/text item via unique IDs.
All files are zipped with a manifest, README, and metadata schema, ready for upload to the repository.

task_reasoner.internal_reasoning: I broke down the complex archival task into discrete, logically ordered steps that cover data collection, standardization, database creation, typological analysis, alignment, cross-referencing, consistency checks, metadata compilation, and final packaging, ensuring each requirement is addressed.

DOMAIN: computational_linguistics
TASK: Gather linguistic field recordings, interlinear glossed texts, and paradigm elicitation data from a Papuan language with no prior written documentation and fewer than 200 speakers, sourced from 6 fieldworkers using 4 different transcription conventions (IPA narrow, IPA broad, practical orthography, ad-hoc romanization). Structure the materials into an ELAR/PARADISEC-compatible archive with FLEx-interoperable lexical database, grammatical sketch organized by typological feature (morphophonology, argument structure, clause combining, and information structure), and curated text collection with time-aligned audio. Polish with cross-references between lexical entries and text attestations, consistency validation of morpheme segmentation across transcribers, and a metadata layer capturing speaker consent status, recording conditions, and dialectal variation notes.



Think carefully about how to solve this task. For each step:
1. State what you need to do
2. Explain WHY this step is necessary
3. Describe the expected output
